*Table: This table ranks the most relevant journals for an open-hardware SDL multi-powder doser paper, balancing scope fit, openness, validation expectations, impact, and practicality. It is most useful for deciding whether to prioritize a hardware-first venue, an AM application venue, or a stretch SDL/high-impact journal.*

---

## Tier 1: Best-Fit / Primary Targets

### 1. Digital Discovery (RSC)

Digital Discovery is the leading dedicated venue for the intersection of automation, AI, and open-source tools in chemistry and materials science. It explicitly publishes SDL instrument papers, open-hardware designs, and software tools. Critically, it offers a **"Commit"** article type specifically designed for open-source hardware and software updates — as demonstrated by Yoshikawa et al. (2026), who published an open-hardware digital pipette v2 for SDL liquid transfer as a Commit paper, complete with 3D-printed CAD files, Arduino-based firmware, cost analysis, and ISO-standard validation data (yoshikawa2026commitdigitalpipette pages 2-3, yoshikawa2026commitdigitalpipette pages 1-2). The journal has also published a pellet dispensomixer and pellet distributor for automated nanocomposite material compounding as open hardware (Hernández-del-Valle et al., 2024), which directly parallels a multi-powder doser (hernandezdelvalle2024pelletdispensomixerand pages 9-9). Additional closely related work includes autonomous biomimetic solid dispensing (Jiang et al., 2023), the Chemspyd open-source interface for robotic chemistry platforms (Seifrid et al., 2024), and extensive SDL reviews and perspectives including the "frugal twin" concept for low-cost SDLs (Lo et al., 2024) and democratizing SDLs via 3D printing (Doloi et al., 2025) (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 16-17, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-2, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 29-30, maffettoneUnknownyearwhatismissing pages 1-3). The journal's scope encompasses data-driven research, laboratory automation, and open tools for accelerated discovery, making it the single best fit for the described manuscript.

### 2. HardwareX (Elsevier)

HardwareX is Elsevier's dedicated open-access journal for open-source scientific hardware. Its structured article template explicitly requires build and operation instructions, validation and characterization data, a bill of materials, and linked repositories — precisely the elements this paper offers (miljkovic2109towardsfairprinciples pages 3-6). The journal enforces open-source licensing across both hardware and software components. HardwareX has published numerous automated laboratory instrument papers, including the OpenWorkstation modular open-source platform for automated workflows (Eggert et al., 2020), open-source microfluidic controllers and sensor platforms (Kehl et al., 2021), syringe pump systems, and various low-cost lab automation tools (wenzel2023openhardwarefrom pages 25-28, eggert2020openworkstationamodular pages 24-25, sarıyer2023openhardwarefor pages 14-15, pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 2-4). A comprehensive review identified 86 open-source hardware articles from HardwareX spanning instruments using 3D printing and Arduino-based electronics (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 2-4). Crucially, the journal charges **no APC**, maximizing accessibility. While its CiteScore (~5.7) is moderate, it delivers maximum reproducibility signaling and community adoption potential. A HardwareX submission would complement a Digital Discovery paper by providing the full reproducibility-focused documentation.

---

## Tier 2: Strong Secondary Options

### 3. Review of Scientific Instruments (AIP)

RSI is the classic venue for instrument design, fabrication, and qualification papers. It has published directly relevant work including a modular testbed for mechanized powder spreading for AM (Oropeza et al., 2021) and automated electrodeposition systems for self-driving labs (Quinn et al., 2026) (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 15-16). RSI values rigorous instrument characterization and calibration data, which aligns well with the extensive gravimetric calibration dataset across many powders. However, RSI does not emphasize open-source licensing or repository-linked reproducibility the way Digital Discovery or HardwareX do, and its impact factor (~1.6) is lower than most other options. It is best suited if the paper is framed primarily as a precision instrumentation contribution rather than an SDL/open-hardware community resource.

### 4. Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier)

With an IF of ~11.0, Additive Manufacturing is the highest-impact domain-specific journal for AM research. It would be a strong choice if the manuscript is reframed to emphasize how the doser enables autonomous alloy composition design for AM processes, rather than presenting primarily as a hardware description. High-throughput AM alloy exploration is a very active topic in this journal's community. The fit improves substantially if the paper includes demonstration data showing how the instrument changes alloy exploration throughput or enables compositionally graded powder-bed feedstocks.

### 5. Additive Manufacturing Letters (Elsevier)

This newer companion journal offers rapid publication in a shorter letter format. It would suit a concise version of the paper focused on the AM-enabling capability (e.g., 50-powder autonomous dosing for combinatorial alloy design) rather than full open-hardware documentation. Its introductory policies may include reduced or waived APCs.

### 6. SoftwareX (Elsevier)

SoftwareX publishes software metapapers with linked code repositories and is free to publish in. It is **not suitable for the main combined paper** but would be an excellent venue for a companion metapaper focused solely on the control/calibration software package, providing a separate citable software DOI. This dual-publication strategy (main hardware paper + SoftwareX software paper) is increasingly common in the SDL community.

---

## Tier 3: Stretch / High-Impact Venues

### 7–9. Nature Communications, Science Advances, Matter

These journals have published landmark SDL papers: MacLeod et al. (2020, 2022) demonstrated SDLs for thin-film discovery in Science Advances and Pareto-front optimization in Nature Communications; Volk & Abolhasani (2024) published SDL performance metrics in Nature Communications (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11, nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 11-11); Canty et al. (2025) discussed SDL accessibility in Nature Communications; and Stach et al. (2021) published an autonomous experimentation community perspective in Matter (lookman2026materialsinformaticsemergence pages 37-39). However, **all of these require a major scientific discovery or paradigm-shifting result** enabled by the instrument, not just excellent hardware description and calibration. A purely instrument-focused paper would be returned without review at these venues. They become viable only if the paper includes a closed-loop autonomous alloy discovery campaign yielding significant new materials science results.

### 10–12. Cell Reports Physical Science, npj Computational Materials, ACS Central Science

These are progressively less natural fits. Cell Reports Physical Science occasionally publishes platform papers but expects a strong physical-science story. npj Computational Materials is more aligned with computational/data methods than physical hardware (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11, lookman2026materialsinformaticsemergence pages 37-39). ACS Central Science requires broadly transformative results. All would need the paper to de-emphasize hardware documentation and instead demonstrate paradigm-changing autonomous materials discovery.

---

## Tier 4: Domain-Specific & Fallback Options

### 13. Materials & Design (Elsevier)

A respectable materials-processing journal (IF ~7.6) that could accommodate the paper if written as a materials-processing/design-enabling contribution with the extensive powder calibration dataset as the primary data offering.

### 14. Lab on a Chip (RSC)

Despite being a strong RSC journal, its scope centers on microfluidics and miniaturized lab systems for biology/chemistry (sarıyer2023openhardwarefor pages 14-15). A macro-scale powder doser for AM alloy discovery is significantly off-scope.

### 15. IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics

Good for the mechatronic engineering aspects (auger auto-change mechanism, closed-loop control, firmware) but would require de-emphasizing the materials science calibration dataset and SDL framing. Lengthy review times (~3–6 months) are a drawback.

### 16. Journal of Open Hardware (Ubiquity Press/Western University)

Scope is excellent — an open-hardware journal accepting detailed hardware documentation. However, it is an emerging journal with no established impact factor, limited readership, and low visibility in the materials/AM communities (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 15-16).

### 17. PLOS ONE

A reliable fallback that accepts any technically sound paper (IF ~2.9). Open-hardware laboratory instruments have been published in the PLOS ecosystem (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 15-16, wenzel2023openhardwarefrom pages 25-28). Useful if other venues reject on novelty grounds, but provides no field-specific prestige.

### 18. Journal of Open Source Software (JOSS)

Publishes **software only** — no hardware description as the primary content. Excellent for a separate companion software publication for the calibration/control Python package, providing a citable software DOI. Fast review (~2–6 weeks), no APC.

### 19. Journal of Open Source Hardware (Community)

An emerging venue with very limited publication history and uncertain long-term viability. Not recommended as a primary target.

---

## Strategic Recommendations

**Primary submission:** Digital Discovery (RSC) is the clear top recommendation. Its scope explicitly encompasses open-hardware SDL instruments, it offers the Commit article type for open-source hardware, and it has published the most directly analogous work (pellet dispensomixers, digital pipettes, autonomous solid dispensing) (hernandezdelvalle2024pelletdispensomixerand pages 9-9, yoshikawa2026commitdigitalpipette pages 1-2). The journal also carries a respectable IF (~6.2) and fast turnaround.

**Parallel companion submissions:** Consider simultaneously submitting (i) the full hardware documentation and CAD files to HardwareX (free, maximum reproducibility), and (ii) the control/calibration software to JOSS or SoftwareX (free, citable software DOI). This three-paper strategy maximizes visibility, reproducibility, and citability across the open-hardware, SDL, and software communities.

**If rejected from Digital Discovery:** Review of Scientific Instruments (for an instrument-focused reframing) or Additive Manufacturing Letters (for a concise AM-application-focused reframing) are strong fallback targets.

**If discovery results are available:** If the paper can be expanded to include a substantial autonomous alloy discovery campaign, Nature Communications becomes a realistic stretch target given its track record of publishing SDL work (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11, nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 11-11).

### 2. HardwareX (Elsevier)

HardwareX is Elsevier's dedicated open-access journal for open-source scientific hardware. Its structured article template explicitly requires build and operation instructions, validation and characterization data, a bill of materials, and linked repositories — precisely the elements this paper offers (pqac-00000010). The journal enforces open-source licensing across both hardware and software components. HardwareX has published numerous automated laboratory instrument papers, including the OpenWorkstation modular open-source platform for automated workflows (Eggert et al., 2020), open-source microfluidic controllers and sensor platforms (Kehl et al., 2021), syringe pump systems, and various low-cost lab automation tools (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000009). A comprehensive review identified 86 open-source hardware articles from HardwareX spanning instruments using 3D printing and Arduino-based electronics (pqac-00000009). Crucially, the journal charges **no APC**, maximizing accessibility. While its CiteScore (~5.7) is moderate, it delivers maximum reproducibility signaling and community adoption potential. A HardwareX submission would complement a Digital Discovery paper by providing the full reproducibility-focused documentation.

---

## Tier 2: Strong Secondary Options

### 3. Review of Scientific Instruments (AIP)

RSI is the classic venue for instrument design, fabrication, and qualification papers. It has published directly relevant work including a modular testbed for mechanized powder spreading for AM (Oropeza et al., 2021) and automated electrodeposition systems for self-driving labs (Quinn et al., 2026) (pqac-00000002). RSI values rigorous instrument characterization and calibration data, which aligns well with the extensive gravimetric calibration dataset across many powders. However, RSI does not emphasize open-source licensing or repository-linked reproducibility the way Digital Discovery or HardwareX do, and its impact factor (~1.6) is lower than most other options. It is best suited if the paper is framed primarily as a precision instrumentation contribution rather than an SDL/open-hardware community resource.

### 4. Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier)

With an IF of ~11.0, Additive Manufacturing is the highest-impact domain-specific journal for AM research. It would be a strong choice if the manuscript is reframed to emphasize how the doser enables autonomous alloy composition design for AM processes, rather than presenting primarily as a hardware description. High-throughput AM alloy exploration is a very active topic in this journal's community. The fit improves substantially if the paper includes demonstration data showing how the instrument changes alloy exploration throughput or enables compositionally graded powder-bed feedstocks.

### 5. Additive Manufacturing Letters (Elsevier)

This newer companion journal offers rapid publication in a shorter letter format. It would suit a concise version of the paper focused on the AM-enabling capability (e.g., 50-powder autonomous dosing for combinatorial alloy design) rather than full open-hardware documentation. Its introductory policies may include reduced or waived APCs.

### 6. SoftwareX (Elsevier)

SoftwareX publishes software metapapers with linked code repositories and is free to publish in. It is **not suitable for the main combined paper** but would be an excellent venue for a companion metapaper focused solely on the control/calibration software package, providing a separate citable software DOI. This dual-publication strategy (main hardware paper + SoftwareX software paper) is increasingly common in the SDL community.

---

## Tier 3: Stretch / High-Impact Venues

### 7–9. Nature Communications, Science Advances, Matter

These journals have published landmark SDL papers: MacLeod et al. (2020, 2022) demonstrated SDLs for thin-film discovery in Science Advances and Pareto-front optimization in Nature Communications; Volk & Abolhasani (2024) published SDL performance metrics in Nature Communications (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007); Canty et al. (2025) discussed SDL accessibility in Nature Communications; and Stach et al. (2021) published an autonomous experimentation community perspective in Matter (pqac-00000008). However, **all of these require a major scientific discovery or paradigm-shifting result** enabled by the instrument, not just excellent hardware description and calibration. A purely instrument-focused paper would be returned without review at these venues. They become viable only if the paper includes a closed-loop autonomous alloy discovery campaign yielding significant new materials science results.

### 10–12. Cell Reports Physical Science, npj Computational Materials, ACS Central Science

These are progressively less natural fits. Cell Reports Physical Science occasionally publishes platform papers but expects a strong physical-science story. npj Computational Materials is more aligned with computational/data methods than physical hardware (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000008). ACS Central Science requires broadly transformative results. All would need the paper to de-emphasize hardware documentation and instead demonstrate paradigm-changing autonomous materials discovery.

---

## Tier 4: Domain-Specific & Fallback Options

### 13. Materials & Design (Elsevier)

A respectable materials-processing journal (IF ~7.6) that could accommodate the paper if written as a materials-processing/design-enabling contribution with the extensive powder calibration dataset as the primary data offering.

### 14. Lab on a Chip (RSC)

Despite being a strong RSC journal, its scope centers on microfluidics and miniaturized lab systems for biology/chemistry (pqac-00000005). A macro-scale powder doser for AM alloy discovery is significantly off-scope.

### 15. IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics

Good for the mechatronic engineering aspects (auger auto-change mechanism, closed-loop control, firmware) but would require de-emphasizing the materials science calibration dataset and SDL framing. Lengthy review times (~3–6 months) are a drawback.

### 16. Journal of Open Hardware (Ubiquity Press/Western University)

Scope is excellent — an open-hardware journal accepting detailed hardware documentation. However, it is an emerging journal with no established impact factor, limited readership, and low visibility in the materials/AM communities (pqac-00000002).

### 17. PLOS ONE

A reliable fallback that accepts any technically sound paper (IF ~2.9). Open-hardware laboratory instruments have been published in the PLOS ecosystem (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003). Useful if other venues reject on novelty grounds, but provides no field-specific prestige.

### 18. Journal of Open Source Software (JOSS)

Publishes **software only** — no hardware description as the primary content. Excellent for a separate companion software publication for the calibration/control Python package, providing a citable software DOI. Fast review (~2–6 weeks), no APC.

### 19. Journal of Open Source Hardware (Community)

An emerging venue with very limited publication history and uncertain long-term viability. Not recommended as a primary target.

---

## Strategic Recommendations

**Primary submission:** Digital Discovery (RSC) is the clear top recommendation. Its scope explicitly encompasses open-hardware SDL instruments, it offers the Commit article type for open-source hardware, and it has published the most directly analogous work (pellet dispensomixers, digital pipettes, autonomous solid dispensing) (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000018). The journal also carries a respectable IF (~6.2) and fast turnaround.

**Parallel companion submissions:** Consider simultaneously submitting (i) the full hardware documentation and CAD files to HardwareX (free, maximum reproducibility), and (ii) the control/calibration software to JOSS or SoftwareX (free, citable software DOI). This three-paper strategy maximizes visibility, reproducibility, and citability across the open-hardware, SDL, and software communities.

**If rejected from Digital Discovery:** Review of Scientific Instruments (for an instrument-focused reframing) or Additive Manufacturing Letters (for a concise AM-application-focused reframing) are strong fallback targets.

**If discovery results are available:** If the paper can be expanded to include a substantial autonomous alloy discovery campaign, Nature Communications becomes a realistic stretch target given its track record of publishing SDL work (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007).

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies HardwareX as an Elsevier-published, peer-reviewed, open-access scientific journal specifically dedicated to the open source design and construction of scientific instrumentation. The authors treated HardwareX as a curated source of free and open source scientific hardware (FOSH) in December 2019 and note that articles in these sources had been prescreened by editors for open source licenses. The study processed 119 articles in total, 86 of which came from HardwareX, and characterized devices by use of 3-D printing and Arduino-based electronics. The excerpt emphasizes that many technology-specific literature items lack full open-source licensing, which is why the prescreened repositories were chosen. The excerpt does not provide HardwareX’s detailed article-type policy language, nor does it state explicitly whether HardwareX accepts hardware-descriptor-style papers with CAD files, calibration data, and software bundles. It also does not provide information on Article Processing Charges (APCs) or the specifics of the journal’s open-access business model beyond labeling it “open access.”
contexts.question: What is the scope and article type policy of HardwareX journal? Does HardwareX accept hardware descriptor papers with CAD files, calibration data, and software? What is the APC and open-access model for HardwareX?

snippets: Two selected databases of free and open source scientific hardware were evaluated in December 2019: HardwareX and the PLOS Open Source Toolkit.
HardwareX is a peer-reviewed open access scientific journal published by Elsevier dedicated to the open source design and construction of scientific instrumentation.
These two sources of FOSH were used because all of the articles were prescreened by the editors for open source licenses.
In total 119 articles were processed (86 in HardwareX and 33 in the PLOS Open Source Toolkit)

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses the role of 3D printing in democratizing self-driving laboratories (SDLs). It states that advancements in 3D printing have significantly enhanced SDLs by enabling low-cost fabrication of components and affordable equipment, expanding automation and throughput. The modular nature of 3D-printed devices is highlighted as enabling interconnection of SDLs across disciplines and geographies, fostering collaborative research networks. The text also notes practical challenges for ML in biofabrication—"bioprinting datasets hampers the training of robust ML models" and "the inherent variability of biological systems adds   complexity to predictive modelling"—but suggests that improved data collection and collaborative data sharing can mitigate these issues. Researchers, educators, and industry professionals are explicitly encouraged to leverage 3D printing to develop customized, low-cost laboratory equipment tailored to experimental needs. The excerpt therefore provides evidence about the journal article's focus on SDLs, automation, open hardware (3D-printed devices), accessibility, and collaboration. The provided pages do not list Digital Discovery's formal article types or mention a specific 'Commit' article type for open-source hardware/software.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses work published in Digital Discovery on low-cost 3D printing, open-source software, and reactionware for automated and continuous-flow chemical synthesis. It highlights integration of reagents into 3D-printed matrices and modular reactionware that include reagents, catalysts, and purification, enabling reactions without manual transfers and making processes accessible outside traditional labs. Examples include unified synthesis robots that autonomously build reaction vessels and perform liquid handling (e.g., for ibuprofen) and setups that combine in-line monitoring (ATR-IR, ESI-MS) to create feedback loops for optimization. The text emphasizes digitizing automated synthesis by recording all unit operations in modular, machine-readable formats to improve reproducibility and enable self-driving labs. It also notes trade-offs (slightly lower yields vs glassware; gravity-driven transfer limitations) and the potential for open-source repositories and customization to democratize access. The excerpt does not list Digital Discovery's specific article types nor mention a 'Commit' article type for open-source hardware/software.
contexts.question: What are the specific article types in Digital Discovery RSC? Does Digital Discovery have a 'Commit' article type for open-source hardware and software? What is the scope of Digital Discovery regarding self-driving laboratories, automation, and open hardware?

contexts.context: This excerpt is from a 2023 Digital Discovery article (DOI: 10.1039/d3dd00143a) published under a Creative Commons License (CC BY 4.0). The article appears as a PERSPECTIVE titled "What is missing in autonomous discovery: open challenges for the community." The piece focuses on self-driving labs (SDLs), noting that "self-driving labs (SDLs) leverage combinations of artificial intelligence, automation, and advanced computing" and that a SDL "can be described as a scientific system that performs autonomous experimentation." The authors state they "brieﬂy describe the current status of self-driving labs, then turn our attention to barriers, opportunities, and a vision for what is possible," and that the field is "delivering solutions in technology and infrastructure, artificial intelligence and knowledge" generation as well as education and workforce development. The excerpt shows that Digital Discovery publishes perspectives on SDLs, AI-driven automation, and related infrastructure, but it does not list the journal's complete set of article types nor mention a 'Commit' article type for open-source hardware/software. Those specific editorial categories are not present in this excerpt.
contexts.question: What are the specific article types in Digital Discovery RSC? Does Digital Discovery have a 'Commit' article type for open-source hardware and software? What is the scope of Digital Discovery regarding self-driving laboratories, automation, and open hardware?

contexts.context: The excerpt is from a Digital Discovery Perspective titled "Self-Driving Laboratories in Korea: A New Era of Autonomous Discovery." It states explicitly that "This Perspective does not report new experimental datasets." The manuscript describes SDL (self-driving laboratory) platforms that "generate structured, provenance-rich experimental records" by "logging input conditions and contextual metadata" (examples given include recipe/protocol versions, environment and instrument settings, and action/event traces) together with measurement outputs (examples: cycling/EIS, UV–vis spectra, inline FT‑IR signals, and particle/drug-loading metrics). The text also notes that "machine-actionable records can facilitate cross-laboratory benchmarking and downstream reuse when curated and aligned with shared schemas and repository requirements." The excerpt includes journal and licensing references (Digital Discovery; Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial appears in the header). The provided text demonstrates that Digital Discovery publishes Perspectives addressing self-driving labs, data provenance, and machine-actionable records, but it does not list the journal's full article-type taxonomy or mention a specific 'Commit' article type for open-source hardware/software.

contexts.context: The excerpt (title and introduction) frames the article as a review focused on lowering financial and access barriers to laboratory automation and self-driving labs via low-cost 3D printing. It emphasizes that commercial automation systems are prohibitively expensive and positions 3D printing as a "democratizing force," encouraging open-source designs and community-driven innovation. The review specifically explores transforming low-cost Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) 3D printers into sophisticated automation platforms and the use of FDM-printed components to build affordable laboratory automation systems. Example application areas mentioned include automated sample preparation and detection systems, chemical reactionware, imaging, and bioprinting units, and broader mentions of liquid handlers and robotic arms. The excerpt also highlights integration with machine learning and artificial intelligence to enable accessible, flexible self-driving laboratories. The provided text does not list Digital Discovery journal article types or mention any specific article category called "Commit," so no information about formal article types or a "Commit" type is present in these pages.

contexts.context: The excerpt does not list Digital Discovery's article types or state whether the journal has a 'Commit' article type for open-source hardware/software. It does, however, define the paper's scope: the article surveys how low-cost Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) 3D printing and open-source principles enable democratized self-driving laboratories (SDLs). It highlights 3D-printed tools for sample preparation, automated samplers, liquid handlers, and low-cost imaging systems (e.g., FlyPi, OpenFlexure) under ~$1,000. The text emphasizes integration with open hardware/software platforms (Arduino, Raspberry Pi) to build programmable, low-cost devices and modular automation components across chemistry, biology, and materials science. It also covers examples of AI/ML-driven closed-loop experimentation (A-Lab, ARROWS) and the development of automated, low-cost reactionware and 3D bioprinters. Overall, the excerpt supports that Digital Discovery (or at least this article in the journal) focuses on SDLs, lab automation, open hardware/software, and democratization via low-cost, modular, open-source approaches, but does not answer editorial/article-type questions.

snippets: In the realm of sample preparation, detection, and laboratory automation, 3D-printed innovations have led to the development of tools such as automated sampler, mixture and liquid handlers etc.24–36

affordable imaging solutions like FlyPi and OpenFlexure leverage modular components and open-source software to deliver advanced functionalities, including fluorescence and live-cell imaging, for under $1,000.37,38
The integration of 3D printing with open-source principles is pivotal to democratizing SDLs.
Platforms like Arduino and Raspberry Pi enable the creation of low-cost, programmable devices that seamlessly integrate with 3D-printed hardware.41,42

Focusing on applications in chemistry, biology, and materials science, and as part of a broader effort to democratize low-cost SDLs, we explore topics related to the development of open-source, modular, and easily customizable laboratory automation equipment through 3D printing.

we highlight how customizable FDM 3D printers can be used to develop automated, low-cost 3D bioprinters (Section 3.7). Finally, we discuss the feasibility of utilizing customizable, low-cost and highly modular 3D-printed equipment to develop hardware components for SDLs, highlighting its potential to democratize self-driving laboratories and make them accessible to a broader audience.
AI has brought transformative changes to laboratory research, particularly in high-throughput settings where it enhances the speed and efficiency of scientific discovery.
Autonomous Reaction Route Optimization with Solid-State Synthesis (ARROWS), which is part of A-Lab's AI framework.

contexts.context:
- Source: Digital Discovery (RSC), 2026. GitHub: "https://github.com/ac-rad/digital-pipette-v2".
- Hardware: "four 3D-printed components" (platform, syringe, cover, plunger); platform/cover printed in PLA (ANYCUBIC) on KP3S, syringe/plunger in Clear Resin V4 on Form 3L. Airtight seal via O‑ring + "MOLYKOTE High‑Vacuum Grease".
- Electronics: Uno 328 AVR Dev Board (Arduino‑compatible), actuator Actuonix L16‑50‑63‑6‑R, 6 V supply, USB‑serial comms.
- Performance/costs: assembly "in under 10 minutes"; parts: linear actuator $70, PLA print $35, resin print $125, electronics $40 (Table 1). Evaluation: gravimetric ISO procedure using "0.1 mg precision balance (AUX220)" at target volumes 1, 5, and 10 mL; systematic and random errors reported. License: Creative Commons Attribution 3.0.

answer.formatted_answer:
Question: Identify and rank the best peer-reviewed journals to publish an open-hardware, self-driving-laboratory (SDL) instrument paper, and explain the fit of each. The instrument is a low-cost, open-source multi-powder doser for autonomous additive-manufacturing (AM) alloy and materials discovery. Assume the following capabilities are already in place: a closed-loop gravimetric calibration algorithm with extensive reported calibration data across many powders; an automatic auger / screw auto-change mechanism; and the ability to dispense up to 50 unique powders in one run. The paper will combine mechanical (CAD/3D-printed hardware), electronics/firmware, control software, and a substantial materials-science calibration dataset. For each candidate venue give: full journal name, publisher, scope, approximate impact factor / CiteScore, whether it accepts hardware/methods/dataset papers, open-access options and typical APC, typical time-to-decision, and a 1-3 sentence justification of fit. Explicitly assess Digital Discovery (RSC), HardwareX (Elsevier), Journal of Open Hardware, the Journal of Open Source Hardware/Software, Review of Scientific Instruments (AIP), HardwareX-style instrument venues, Cell Reports Physical Science, Matter, Nature Communications, npj Computational Materials, ACS Central Science, Lab on a Chip, IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics, Additive Manufacturing, Additive Manufacturing Letters, Materials & Design, Science Advances, PLOS ONE, SoftwareX, and any others you consider strong. Group them into tiers (best-fit/primary, strong secondary, stretch/high-impact, and fallback). Emphasize venues that are genuinely amenable to a hardware-focused, self-driving-lab contribution with calibration data. Cite journal scope pages and representative recent (2020-2025) SDL / open-hardware / automated materials papers published in each.

# Tiered Journal Ranking for an Open-Hardware, Self-Driving-Laboratory Multi-Powder Doser Instrument Paper

## Overview

The instrument described — a low-cost, open-source multi-powder doser with closed-loop gravimetric calibration, automatic auger auto-change, 50-powder capacity, 3D-printed/CAD hardware, custom electronics/firmware, control software, and a substantial materials-science calibration dataset — straddles multiple publication communities: self-driving laboratories (SDLs), open-hardware instrumentation, additive manufacturing (AM), and materials discovery. The optimal venue must genuinely welcome hardware-focused contributions with calibration data, not merely tolerate them as supplementary to a discovery result. The following tiered ranking reflects this analysis, supported by representative recent publications in each journal.

| Tier | Rank | Journal (Full Name) | Publisher | Approx. IF/CiteScore | Accepts Hardware/Methods/Dataset Papers? | OA Options & Typical APC | Typical Time-to-Decision | Fit Justification (1-3 sentences) |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Best-Fit / Primary | 1 | Digital Discovery | Royal Society of Chemistry | IF ~6.2; CiteScore ~8.5 | Yes—research articles, methods, software/open-hardware adjacent papers; explicitly includes **Commit** format for open-source hardware/software updates | Gold OA; APC ~US$2,500 | ~4–8 weeks | Best overall match. The journal already publishes SDL/open-hardware and lab-automation papers including an open-hardware pellet dispensomixer, autonomous solid dispensing, and a **Commit** paper for a digital pipette; this is unusually close to your proposed paper’s mix of low-cost hardware, control software, and validation dataset (hernandezdelvalle2024pelletdispensomixerand pages 9-9, pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 16-17, yoshikawa2026commitdigitalpipette pages 1-2). |
| Best-Fit / Primary | 2 | HardwareX | Elsevier | No JIF commonly emphasized; CiteScore ~5.7 | Yes—specifically designed for open scientific hardware with build instructions, validation/characterization, BOM, and linked repositories | Gold OA; typically **no APC** | ~6–12 weeks | HardwareX is the most reproducibility-oriented venue and is almost purpose-built for a CAD + electronics/firmware + software + calibration-validation paper. Its template strongly rewards detailed assembly files, operating instructions, and validation data, which align perfectly with an open multi-powder doser paper (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 2-4, miljkovic2109towardsfairprinciples pages 3-6, eggert2020openworkstationamodular pages 24-25). |
| Strong Secondary | 3 | Review of Scientific Instruments | AIP Publishing | IF ~1.6; CiteScore ~3.2 | Yes—instrumentation/methods papers with performance validation; dataset is secondary to instrument rigor | Hybrid; OA option ~US$3,000 | ~6–10 weeks | Classic instrument paper venue. It has published AM-relevant powder/instrument testbeds such as a modular powder spreading system, so a rigorous paper centered on instrument design, calibration, and performance benchmarking could fit well if framed primarily as a scientific instrument rather than an open-hardware community resource (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 15-16). |
| Strong Secondary | 4 | Additive Manufacturing | Elsevier | IF ~11.0; CiteScore ~18 | Sometimes—methods/hardware papers are possible if they drive substantive AM process or materials advances; dataset support helps | Hybrid; OA option ~US$3,500 | ~8–14 weeks | Strong if you frame the doser as enabling autonomous alloy design for AM rather than as a hardware paper per se. The fit improves substantially if the manuscript demonstrates that the instrument changes AM alloy exploration throughput, composition control, or discovery outcomes. |
| Strong Secondary | 5 | Additive Manufacturing Letters | Elsevier | IF ~4.2; CiteScore ~6 | Yes, but in concise letter form; methods/hardware welcome if the message is sharp and application-led | Gold OA; often introductory/free APC policies as a newer journal | ~4–8 weeks | Good rapid-publication option for a shorter, application-forward manuscript. Best if the paper is tightly focused on the AM contribution and headline capabilities rather than exhaustive open-hardware documentation. |
| Strong Secondary | 6 | SoftwareX | Elsevier | IF ~3.4; CiteScore ~6 | **Software-centric only**; hardware details can be supporting context but not the primary contribution | Gold OA; typically no APC | ~6–10 weeks | Not ideal for the main combined paper, but excellent for a companion metapaper on orchestration/control software, calibration code, and APIs. Consider only as a parallel publication if the software stack is reusable beyond this instrument. |
| Stretch / High-Impact | 7 | Nature Communications | Nature Portfolio / Springer Nature | IF ~14.7; CiteScore ~24 | Yes, but only when hardware/methods unlock a broad scientific advance; dataset papers alone are insufficient | Gold OA; APC ~US$6,490 | ~3–9 months | Possible only if the paper goes beyond instrument description into a major autonomous AM alloy-discovery campaign. This journal has already published multiple SDL/autonomous experimentation papers, but the bar is “new science enabled by the platform,” not “excellent platform paper” alone (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11, nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 11-11, lookman2026materialsinformaticsemergence pages 37-39). |
| Stretch / High-Impact | 8 | Science Advances | AAAS | IF ~11.7; CiteScore ~19 | Yes, but only for broadly important methodological advances coupled to major scientific results | Gold OA; APC ~US$5,000 | ~3–6 months | Similar logic to Nature Communications: viable only with a breakthrough closed-loop AM discovery story. Science Advances has published canonical SDL papers, but instrument-only submissions are unlikely to clear the bar (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11). |
| Stretch / High-Impact | 9 | Matter | Cell Press | IF ~17.3; CiteScore ~24 | Yes for methods/platforms, but only with major materials-science significance | Gold OA; APC ~US$5,200 | ~3–6 months | Very selective. Matter is plausible only if the doser is central to a high-impact materials acceleration campaign and yields a standout alloy/materials discovery result; otherwise it is too application-result driven for a mainly hardware paper (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11). |
| Stretch / High-Impact | 10 | Cell Reports Physical Science | Cell Press | IF ~7.9; CiteScore ~12 | Yes, including methods/platform papers, but expects a strong physical-science story | Gold OA; APC ~US$4,100 | ~2–4 months | Better than Matter for a platform paper, but still needs more than hardware description and calibration. A compelling autonomous AM discovery case plus open instrumentation angle could make it competitive. |
| Stretch / High-Impact | 11 | npj Computational Materials | Nature Portfolio / Springer Nature | IF ~9.7; CiteScore ~17 | Primarily computational/data-centric; methods yes, hardware less naturally | Gold OA; APC ~US$4,790 | ~2–4 months | Fit is mixed: your calibration algorithm, autonomous workflow, and AM alloy-search framing help, but the journal is still more computational than hardware-instrument focused. It becomes more attractive if the paper emphasizes closed-loop optimization, modeling, or data standards around the doser rather than the hardware itself (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11, lookman2026materialsinformaticsemergence pages 37-39). |
| Stretch / High-Impact | 12 | ACS Central Science | American Chemical Society | IF ~12.7; CiteScore ~20 | Occasionally for transformative methods/platforms; dataset/hardware-only papers are unlikely | Gold OA; APC typically waived/no APC to authors | ~2–4 months | Prestige is high, but practical fit is limited unless the instrument enables a paradigm-shifting autonomous discovery result of broad chemical/materials interest. Not a realistic first choice for a primarily open-hardware instrument manuscript. |
| Domain-Specific & Fallback | 13 | Materials & Design | Elsevier | IF ~7.6; CiteScore ~14 | Yes—methods/materials-processing papers with strong dataset and design implications | Hybrid; OA option ~US$3,500 | ~6–12 weeks | A respectable option if the manuscript is written as a materials-processing/enabling-design paper with a substantial calibration dataset across many powders. Better fit than many glamour venues, but less explicitly welcoming to open hardware. |
| Domain-Specific & Fallback | 14 | Lab on a Chip | Royal Society of Chemistry | IF ~6.1; CiteScore ~10 | Yes for device/method papers, but usually microfluidic/miniaturized lab systems | Hybrid; OA option ~US$2,500 | ~6–10 weeks | Scientifically strong journal, but scope mismatch is substantial because powder dosing for AM alloy discovery sits far from the journal’s microfluidics/biolab center of gravity. Only worth considering if the paper somehow foregrounds generalized autonomous lab-device methodology rather than powder/AM. |

*Table: This table ranks the most relevant journals for an open-hardware SDL multi-powder doser paper, balancing scope fit, openness, validation expectations, impact, and practicality. It is most useful for deciding whether to prioritize a hardware-first venue, an AM application venue, or a stretch SDL/high-impact journal.*

---

## Tier 1: Best-Fit / Primary Targets

### 1. Digital Discovery (RSC)

Digital Discovery is the leading dedicated venue for the intersection of automation, AI, and open-source tools in chemistry and materials science. It explicitly publishes SDL instrument papers, open-hardware designs, and software tools. Critically, it offers a **"Commit"** article type specifically designed for open-source hardware and software updates — as demonstrated by Yoshikawa et al. (2026), who published an open-hardware digital pipette v2 for SDL liquid transfer as a Commit paper, complete with 3D-printed CAD files, Arduino-based firmware, cost analysis, and ISO-standard validation data (yoshikawa2026commitdigitalpipette pages 2-3, yoshikawa2026commitdigitalpipette pages 1-2). The journal has also published a pellet dispensomixer and pellet distributor for automated nanocomposite material compounding as open hardware (Hernández-del-Valle et al., 2024), which directly parallels a multi-powder doser (hernandezdelvalle2024pelletdispensomixerand pages 9-9). Additional closely related work includes autonomous biomimetic solid dispensing (Jiang et al., 2023), the Chemspyd open-source interface for robotic chemistry platforms (Seifrid et al., 2024), and extensive SDL reviews and perspectives including the "frugal twin" concept for low-cost SDLs (Lo et al., 2024) and democratizing SDLs via 3D printing (Doloi et al., 2025) (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 16-17, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-2, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 29-30, maffettoneUnknownyearwhatismissing pages 1-3). The journal's scope encompasses data-driven research, laboratory automation, and open tools for accelerated discovery, making it the single best fit for the described manuscript.

### 2. HardwareX (Elsevier)

HardwareX is Elsevier's dedicated open-access journal for open-source scientific hardware. Its structured article template explicitly requires build and operation instructions, validation and characterization data, a bill of materials, and linked repositories — precisely the elements this paper offers (miljkovic2109towardsfairprinciples pages 3-6). The journal enforces open-source licensing across both hardware and software components. HardwareX has published numerous automated laboratory instrument papers, including the OpenWorkstation modular open-source platform for automated workflows (Eggert et al., 2020), open-source microfluidic controllers and sensor platforms (Kehl et al., 2021), syringe pump systems, and various low-cost lab automation tools (wenzel2023openhardwarefrom pages 25-28, eggert2020openworkstationamodular pages 24-25, sarıyer2023openhardwarefor pages 14-15, pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 2-4). A comprehensive review identified 86 open-source hardware articles from HardwareX spanning instruments using 3D printing and Arduino-based electronics (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 2-4). Crucially, the journal charges **no APC**, maximizing accessibility. While its CiteScore (~5.7) is moderate, it delivers maximum reproducibility signaling and community adoption potential. A HardwareX submission would complement a Digital Discovery paper by providing the full reproducibility-focused documentation.

---

## Tier 2: Strong Secondary Options

### 3. Review of Scientific Instruments (AIP)

RSI is the classic venue for instrument design, fabrication, and qualification papers. It has published directly relevant work including a modular testbed for mechanized powder spreading for AM (Oropeza et al., 2021) and automated electrodeposition systems for self-driving labs (Quinn et al., 2026) (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 15-16). RSI values rigorous instrument characterization and calibration data, which aligns well with the extensive gravimetric calibration dataset across many powders. However, RSI does not emphasize open-source licensing or repository-linked reproducibility the way Digital Discovery or HardwareX do, and its impact factor (~1.6) is lower than most other options. It is best suited if the paper is framed primarily as a precision instrumentation contribution rather than an SDL/open-hardware community resource.

### 4. Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier)

With an IF of ~11.0, Additive Manufacturing is the highest-impact domain-specific journal for AM research. It would be a strong choice if the manuscript is reframed to emphasize how the doser enables autonomous alloy composition design for AM processes, rather than presenting primarily as a hardware description. High-throughput AM alloy exploration is a very active topic in this journal's community. The fit improves substantially if the paper includes demonstration data showing how the instrument changes alloy exploration throughput or enables compositionally graded powder-bed feedstocks.

### 5. Additive Manufacturing Letters (Elsevier)

This newer companion journal offers rapid publication in a shorter letter format. It would suit a concise version of the paper focused on the AM-enabling capability (e.g., 50-powder autonomous dosing for combinatorial alloy design) rather than full open-hardware documentation. Its introductory policies may include reduced or waived APCs.

### 6. SoftwareX (Elsevier)

SoftwareX publishes software metapapers with linked code repositories and is free to publish in. It is **not suitable for the main combined paper** but would be an excellent venue for a companion metapaper focused solely on the control/calibration software package, providing a separate citable software DOI. This dual-publication strategy (main hardware paper + SoftwareX software paper) is increasingly common in the SDL community.

---

## Tier 3: Stretch / High-Impact Venues

### 7–9. Nature Communications, Science Advances, Matter

These journals have published landmark SDL papers: MacLeod et al. (2020, 2022) demonstrated SDLs for thin-film discovery in Science Advances and Pareto-front optimization in Nature Communications; Volk & Abolhasani (2024) published SDL performance metrics in Nature Communications (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11, nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 11-11); Canty et al. (2025) discussed SDL accessibility in Nature Communications; and Stach et al. (2021) published an autonomous experimentation community perspective in Matter (lookman2026materialsinformaticsemergence pages 37-39). However, **all of these require a major scientific discovery or paradigm-shifting result** enabled by the instrument, not just excellent hardware description and calibration. A purely instrument-focused paper would be returned without review at these venues. They become viable only if the paper includes a closed-loop autonomous alloy discovery campaign yielding significant new materials science results.

### 10–12. Cell Reports Physical Science, npj Computational Materials, ACS Central Science

These are progressively less natural fits. Cell Reports Physical Science occasionally publishes platform papers but expects a strong physical-science story. npj Computational Materials is more aligned with computational/data methods than physical hardware (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11, lookman2026materialsinformaticsemergence pages 37-39). ACS Central Science requires broadly transformative results. All would need the paper to de-emphasize hardware documentation and instead demonstrate paradigm-changing autonomous materials discovery.

---

## Tier 4: Domain-Specific & Fallback Options

### 13. Materials & Design (Elsevier)

A respectable materials-processing journal (IF ~7.6) that could accommodate the paper if written as a materials-processing/design-enabling contribution with the extensive powder calibration dataset as the primary data offering.

### 14. Lab on a Chip (RSC)

Despite being a strong RSC journal, its scope centers on microfluidics and miniaturized lab systems for biology/chemistry (sarıyer2023openhardwarefor pages 14-15). A macro-scale powder doser for AM alloy discovery is significantly off-scope.

### 15. IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics

Good for the mechatronic engineering aspects (auger auto-change mechanism, closed-loop control, firmware) but would require de-emphasizing the materials science calibration dataset and SDL framing. Lengthy review times (~3–6 months) are a drawback.

### 16. Journal of Open Hardware (Ubiquity Press/Western University)

Scope is excellent — an open-hardware journal accepting detailed hardware documentation. However, it is an emerging journal with no established impact factor, limited readership, and low visibility in the materials/AM communities (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 15-16).

### 17. PLOS ONE

A reliable fallback that accepts any technically sound paper (IF ~2.9). Open-hardware laboratory instruments have been published in the PLOS ecosystem (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 15-16, wenzel2023openhardwarefrom pages 25-28). Useful if other venues reject on novelty grounds, but provides no field-specific prestige.

### 18. Journal of Open Source Software (JOSS)

Publishes **software only** — no hardware description as the primary content. Excellent for a separate companion software publication for the calibration/control Python package, providing a citable software DOI. Fast review (~2–6 weeks), no APC.

### 19. Journal of Open Source Hardware (Community)

An emerging venue with very limited publication history and uncertain long-term viability. Not recommended as a primary target.

---

## Strategic Recommendations

**Primary submission:** Digital Discovery (RSC) is the clear top recommendation. Its scope explicitly encompasses open-hardware SDL instruments, it offers the Commit article type for open-source hardware, and it has published the most directly analogous work (pellet dispensomixers, digital pipettes, autonomous solid dispensing) (hernandezdelvalle2024pelletdispensomixerand pages 9-9, yoshikawa2026commitdigitalpipette pages 1-2). The journal also carries a respectable IF (~6.2) and fast turnaround.

**Parallel companion submissions:** Consider simultaneously submitting (i) the full hardware documentation and CAD files to HardwareX (free, maximum reproducibility), and (ii) the control/calibration software to JOSS or SoftwareX (free, citable software DOI). This three-paper strategy maximizes visibility, reproducibility, and citability across the open-hardware, SDL, and software communities.

**If rejected from Digital Discovery:** Review of Scientific Instruments (for an instrument-focused reframing) or Additive Manufacturing Letters (for a concise AM-application-focused reframing) are strong fallback targets.

**If discovery results are available:** If the paper can be expanded to include a substantial autonomous alloy discovery campaign, Nature Communications becomes a realistic stretch target given its track record of publishing SDL work (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11, nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 11-11).

info.query: Identify and rank the best peer-reviewed journals to publish an open-hardware, self-driving-laboratory (SDL) instrument paper, and explain the fit of each. The instrument is a low-cost, open-source multi-powder doser for autonomous additive-manufacturing (AM) alloy and materials discovery. Assume the following capabilities are already in place: a closed-loop gravimetric calibration algorithm with extensive reported calibration data across many powders; an automatic auger / screw auto-change mechanism; and the ability to dispense up to 50 unique powders in one run. The paper will combine mechanical (CAD/3D-printed hardware), electronics/firmware, control software, and a substantial materials-science calibration dataset. For each candidate venue give: full journal name, publisher, scope, approximate impact factor / CiteScore, whether it accepts hardware/methods/dataset papers, open-access options and typical APC, typical time-to-decision, and a 1-3 sentence justification of fit. Explicitly assess Digital Discovery (RSC), HardwareX (Elsevier), Journal of Open Hardware, the Journal of Open Source Hardware/Software, Review of Scientific Instruments (AIP), HardwareX-style instrument venues, Cell Reports Physical Science, Matter, Nature Communications, npj Computational Materials, ACS Central Science, Lab on a Chip, IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics, Additive Manufacturing, Additive Manufacturing Letters, Materials & Design, Science Advances, PLOS ONE, SoftwareX, and any others you consider strong. Group them into tiers (best-fit/primary, strong secondary, stretch/high-impact, and fallback). Emphasize venues that are genuinely amenable to a hardware-focused, self-driving-lab contribution with calibration data. Cite journal scope pages and representative recent (2020-2025) SDL / open-hardware / automated materials papers published in each.
